content:
4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

We all know the feeling of writing code that works, but runs just a little too slow. Usually, we look for complex solutions: learning a new framework, upgrading our hardware or rewriting the whole thing from scratch.
But recently, while working on a C++ performance lab, I discovered that one of the biggest bottlenecks in modern programming has nothing to do with the math you are writing. It has everything to do with how your computer reads.
By simply swapping two lines of code, I made a massive calculation ~23% faster. Here’s the story:
The Setup: A Billion Operations
For one of my experiments, I decided to optimize a Matrix Multiplication algorithm. If you aren’t a math nerd, don’t worry — a matrix is just a giant grid of numbers, like a massive Excel spreadsheet. To multiply two of these grids together, the computer has to match up rows from the first grid with columns from the second grid, multiply them and add them up.
To test the physical limits of my CPU, I created two massive grids: 1,000 rows by 1,000 columns. To multiply them together, my computer had to perform 1 billion mathematical operations. I wrote the standard textbook code for this using three nested loops (an i loop, a j loop, and a k loop). I hit run.
The Baseline Time: 0.88 seconds.
Not bad for a billion calculations. But I knew it could be faster.
The Problem: Reading a Book Vertically
To understand why my code was slow, we have to look at how a computer’s brain (the CPU) talks to its short-term memory (the RAM).
When a CPU wants a piece of data, it doesn’t just grab one number. It assumes that if you want a specific number, you will probably want the numbers right next to it too. So, it grabs a whole chunk of data horizontally and stores it in an ultra-fast, tiny vault right next to the processor called the L1 Cache.
Imagine reading a physical book. When you look at a page, your eyes naturally read horizontally, left to right, sentence by sentence. This is fast and efficient.
But behind the scenes, my algorithm was forcing the computer to read the second grid vertically. Because of how the math was structured, it was pulling a number from row 1, jumping down to row 2, jumping down to row 3 and so on.
In our book analogy, this is like reading the first word of page 1, flipping the page to read the first word of page 2, flipping the page again to read the first word of page 3.
Not only is flipping the page slow, but you are completely wasting the rest of the page! The CPU was doing all the hard work to fetch a horizontal chunk of data into its ultra-fast L1 Cache, but my code only used one number from that chunk before jumping hundreds of addresses away.
Because the CPU was jumping around, it kept looking in its L1 Cache and realizing the data it needed wasn’t there. This is called a Cache Miss. Millions of times per second, the CPU had to pause the math, walk all the way back to the slow RAM and fetch a new chunk of numbers.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why loop order matters: Reading vertically (left) throws away fetched data, causing a Cache Miss. A horizontal read (right) uses the entire cache line.The Fix: The Magic Swap
The solution was almost too simple to believe. I didn’t change the math. I didn’t delete any operations.
I just swapped the order of the inner two loops. I changed my code from i-j-k to i-k-j.
By making j (the horizontal column tracker) the innermost loop, it changed the way the math was assembled. Instead of trying to calculate one final number at a time (which required scanning vertically down the grid), the computer now calculated a partial result for an entire row at once.
Physically, this meant the computer was finally reading both grids horizontally, left to right. When the CPU fetched a chunk of memory into the L1 Cache, my code actually used every single number in that chunk before asking for more. No more wasted memory. No more Cache Misses.
I hit run again on my 1-billion operation test.
The New Time: 0.68 seconds.
That is a massive ~23% speedup just by swapping two lines of text.
The Golden Rule of Performance
This experiment proves a concept called Spatial Locality. In simple terms: data that is used together should live together in memory and be read sequentially.
Modern computers are insanely fast at doing math. Often, your code isn’t slow because the processor is struggling to calculate; your code is slow because the processor is constantly waiting around for the data to arrive.
If you align your code to read memory sequentially — the way the silicon hardware was physically built to read it — you unlock performance that no software trick can match.
But I wanted to go faster. Now that my single core was reading memory perfectly, it was time to wake up other 19 cores on my machine.
(In next Part, we’ll look at why trying to use all 20 cores at once caused a massive traffic jam and why multi-threading isn’t the magic bullet we think it is).
